questions.question: Explain the speed-size-cost trade-off in memory systems. Compare SRAM and DRAM technologies. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Faster memory (SRAM) is more expensive per bit and available in smaller capacities.
Cheaper memory (DRAM) is slower but offers higher density.
SRAM: 6 transistors/bit, no refresh, 1-10 ns, used for cache.
DRAM: 1 transistor + 1 capacitor/bit, needs refresh, 50-100 ns, used for main memory.
No single technology satisfies all three requirements simultaneously.

questions.question: Explain the memory hierarchy with a diagram. Describe the principle of locality and derive the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) formula with a numerical example. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Memory hierarchy: Registers (fastest) -> L1/L2/L3 Cache (SRAM) -> Main Memory (DRAM) -> Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD, slowest). Each level trades speed for capacity and lower cost.
Temporal locality: Recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again soon (e.g., loop counters).
Spatial locality: Nearby memory locations are likely to be accessed soon (e.g., array traversal).
Cache hit: Data found in cache (fast). Cache miss: Data not in cache, must fetch from main memory (slow).
AMAT = h x t_cache + (1-h) x t_memory, where h = hit rate.
Example: cache time = 5 ns, memory time = 60 ns, hit rate = 95%. AMAT = 0.95 x 5 + 0.05 x 60 = 4.75 + 3.0 = 7.75 ns.
Average cost per bit = (C1 x S1 + C2 x S2) / (S1 + S2). Since main memory is much larger, average cost approaches the cheaper DRAM cost.
